question: Compare MVC, MVVM, VIPER, and TCA on iOS.
answer:
Common iOS architectures and what they optimize for:

• MVC (Massive View Controller) — Apple's default. UIViewController owns view + business logic. Simple, but VC bloats; hard to test.
• MVVM — extract presentation logic into ViewModel that exposes observable state. View binds to VM. Common with SwiftUI/Combine. Testable VM, but no answer to navigation.
• VIPER — View, Interactor, Presenter, Entity, Router. Strict separation; verbose; classic in large enterprise UIKit apps.
• Coordinators — augment any pattern by extracting navigation into Coordinator objects. Keeps VCs / VMs ignorant of routing.
• TCA (The Composable Architecture) — Pointfree's Redux-flavored library: State, Action, Reducer, Effect, Store. Pure functions, time-travel debugging, exhaustive tests, ergonomic SwiftUI integration.
• Clean Swift / VIP — Apple's MVC + use-case layers; less fashionable today.

No silver bullet — pick by team size, testability needs, and SwiftUI vs UIKit.

question: How do you avoid 'Massive View Controller' in UIKit?
answer:
Strategies to keep UIViewController lean:

• Extract presentation logic into a ViewModel — VC binds + forwards events.
• Move table/collection data sources into separate types — keep dataSource / delegate code out of VC.
• Coordinators — pull navigation/presentation out of VC.
• Service layer — networking, persistence as injected protocols, not inline calls.
• Composition — build complex screens from child VCs (`addChild` / `view.addSubview` / `didMove(toParent:)`).
• Dependency injection — pass collaborators via init; testable mocks for unit tests.
• Use protocol-oriented design for testability rather than singletons.

The goal: VC orchestrates, others do the work. A 200-line VC is acceptable; a 2000-line VC is a smell.

question: What is the Coordinator pattern? When is it worth using?
answer:
A Coordinator is an object responsible for navigation. View controllers (or SwiftUI views) ask the coordinator to navigate; they never push/present each other directly.

Benefits:
• VC reuse — same VC can be used in different flows because flow lives in the coordinator.
• Easier deep linking — coordinator owns the full path.
• Easier testing — VC tests don't have to mock UINavigationController.
• Clear flow boundaries — login flow, onboarding flow, checkout flow each have their own coordinator.

Worth it for medium+ apps with multiple flows. Overkill for small 2-screen apps. SwiftUI alternatives: NavigationStack with typed routes serves the same role.

question: How do you do dependency injection on iOS?
answer:
Common DI strategies on iOS:

• Constructor injection — pass collaborators through `init`. Most explicit, most testable. Default choice.
• Property injection — set after init. Useful for storyboard-instantiated VCs.
• Method injection — pass into the call. Light; fits one-off needs.
• Service locator / DI container — Swinject, Resolver, Factory, Needle. Hide wiring; risk of hidden coupling.
• Environment / @EnvironmentObject (SwiftUI) — DI via the view tree.

For most apps: prefer constructor injection + small composition root that wires everything in `AppDelegate` / `App`. Reach for a container only when wiring becomes painful (10+ services with cyclic-ish dependencies).

question: What is The Composable Architecture (TCA) and what problems does it solve?
answer:
TCA (Pointfree's library) is a Redux-style architecture for SwiftUI/UIKit:

• State — single immutable struct describing the screen.
• Action — enum of every event that can happen.
• Reducer — pure function `(State, Action) -> Effect` that mutates state and emits effects.
• Effect — async work (network, timers) that resolves into more actions.
• Store — holds state + dispatches actions.

Problems it solves:
• Single source of truth: everything goes through one reducer.
• Composability: features can be composed via Reducer combinators; large apps stay tidy.
• Testability: pure reducers + exhaustive `TestStore` lets you replay every action and assert state changes deterministically.
• Side-effect management: structured concurrency wrapped in Effect, with cancellation.

Trade-offs: heavier than MVVM, learning curve, lots of boilerplate (mitigated with @Reducer + @ObservableState macros in 1.0+).

question: How do you organize a large iOS codebase? Modules, frameworks, SPM packages.
answer:
Modularization strategies:

• SPM local packages — split features into local Swift Packages (`Package.swift` with multiple products). Each module compiles independently — fast incremental builds, enforced API boundaries via `public`. Today's recommended default.
• Xcode frameworks — pre-SPM way; still common in legacy apps. Slower incremental than packages.
• XCFrameworks — for distributing prebuilt binary modules to apps that can't build from source (third-party SDKs).

Typical layout:
• `App/` — thin shell that composes all modules.
• `Features/Profile`, `Features/Feed`, `Features/Search` — feature modules.
• `Core/Networking`, `Core/Persistence`, `Core/Logging` — cross-cutting.
• `DesignSystem` — shared UI components, colors, typography.

Benefits: faster builds (parallel module compilation), enforced API boundaries, easier testing, code reuse across multiple apps.
